md: **Trigonometry** is the study of relationships between angles and side lengths in triangles. It started with one simple question: *given an angle in a right triangle, can we predict the ratios of its sides?* The answer turns out to be yes — and it leads to three of the most important functions in all of mathematics.
Pick one of the *non-right* angles in a right triangle and call it $\theta$. Now we name the sides relative to $\theta$:
**Hypotenuse** — the longest side, always across from the right angle.
**Adjacent** — the *other* leg (next to $\theta$, not the hypotenuse).

blocks.title: Switch your perspective
blocks.body: If you pick the *other* non-right angle as $\theta$, the labels for opposite and adjacent swap. The hypotenuse never changes — it's always across from the right angle.

md: Given an angle $\theta$, three ratios of the sides matter:
Try the interactive triangle below. Drag the slider to change $\theta$ — watch the side lengths and the three ratios update together.

The ratios depend *only on the angle*, not on the size of the triangle. Two right triangles with the same angle $\theta$ have the same sin/cos/tan values — even if one is tiny and the other huge. The ratios encode the *shape* of the triangle, not the *size*. That's why they're so useful.

blocks.title: Special angles
blocks.body: Some angles give clean exact values: $\sin 30° = \tfrac{1}{2}$, $\cos 60° = \tfrac{1}{2}$, $\sin 45° = \cos 45° = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$, $\sin 60° = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. These come up *constantly* — worth memorizing.

md: ### Worked example: how tall is the tree?
You stand 20 m from a tree and look up at the top with a measured angle of $35°$. How tall is the tree (above your eye level)?
**Set up.** The horizontal distance ($20$) is *adjacent*; the tree's height is *opposite*. We want opposite, we have adjacent — that calls for the **tangent** ratio:
**Solve.** $\text{height} = 20 \cdot \tan 35° \approx 20 \cdot 0.700 \approx 14$ m. The tree is about 14 meters tall above your eye level.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Every angle $\theta$ in a right triangle determines three ratios: $\sin\theta$ (opp/hyp), $\cos\theta$ (adj/hyp), $\tan\theta$ (opp/adj). SOHCAHTOA is your friend. The ratios depend only on the angle — that's why they're powerful.

md: The **sine** of an angle in a right triangle is the ratio of the side *opposite* the angle to the *hypotenuse*. The sine ratio lets you do two things:
1. **Find a missing side** when you know one side and an angle.
2. **Find a missing angle** when you know two sides (using $\sin^{-1}$).
Pick the angle $\theta$ you care about (not the right angle).
**Hypotenuse** — the longest side, *across from the right angle*. It never changes.

blocks.title: Quick tip
blocks.body: If your unknown is on top of the fraction (opposite), multiply: $\text{opposite} = \text{hyp} \cdot \sin\theta$. If it's on the bottom (hypotenuse), divide: $\text{hyp} = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\sin\theta}$.

md: A right triangle has hypotenuse $12$ and an angle of $35°$. Find the side opposite the $35°$ angle.
**Set up.** Opposite is unknown, hypotenuse is known, angle is known — sine is the right ratio.
A right triangle has opposite side $5$ and hypotenuse $13$. Find $\theta$.
**Set up.** Both opposite and hypotenuse are known.

blocks.title: Two patterns to recognize
blocks.body: When the **angle and hypotenuse** are known and you want the **opposite**, multiply. When **two sides (opp + hyp)** are known and you want the **angle**, take $\sin^{-1}$ of opp/hyp.

md: The **cosine** of an angle in a right triangle is the ratio of the *adjacent* side to the *hypotenuse*. Use cosine whenever your problem involves the **adjacent** side.
The **adjacent** side is the leg that is *next to* $\theta$ — but **not** the hypotenuse. Of the two legs, one touches $\theta$ (adjacent) and one is across from it (opposite).
If the angle changes, the adjacent and opposite *swap*. The hypotenuse never moves.

blocks.title: Sine vs cosine
blocks.body: Both have hypotenuse on the bottom. Sine uses **opposite** (across), cosine uses **adjacent** (touching). Same algebra, different leg.

md: A right triangle has hypotenuse $20$ and an angle of $60°$. Find the side **adjacent** to that angle.
**Set up.** Adjacent unknown, hypotenuse known, angle known → cosine.

The **tangent** of an angle is the ratio of the *opposite* side to the *adjacent* side. Tangent is special — there's no hypotenuse in it. So whenever a problem involves only the *two legs* of the right triangle (and not the hypotenuse), tangent is your tool.

blocks.title: When to choose tangent
blocks.body: If your problem mentions the **two legs** (and not the hypotenuse), use tangent. If it mentions the **hypotenuse and one leg**, use sine or cosine.

md: $\theta = 40°$ and the side **adjacent** to $\theta$ is $10$. Find the **opposite** side.
**Set up.** Opposite is unknown, adjacent is known, hypotenuse not in play → tangent.

blocks.title: Heads up
blocks.body: If the unknown is the **adjacent** side instead, the unknown is in the *denominator*: $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}}$ → $\text{adj} = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\tan\theta}$.

md: When you face a right-triangle problem, the *hard* part isn't doing the algebra — it's choosing the right ratio. SOHCAHTOA is a memory aid; the real skill is matching the problem to the formula.
**Step 1.** Label the sides relative to the angle you're working with: opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse.
**Step 2.** Identify the **two sides** in the problem — the one you know and the one you want.
**Step 3.** Match those two sides to a ratio:

blocks.title: The matching table
blocks.body: **opp + hyp** → use **sin**. **adj + hyp** → use **cos**. **opp + adj** → use **tan**.

md: *A right triangle has hypotenuse $20$. The angle of interest is $\theta = 28°$. Find the side **adjacent** to $\theta$.*
Two sides involved: **adjacent** (unknown) and **hypotenuse** (known). That's adj + hyp → **cosine**.
If the unknown is a **side**, isolate the variable and multiply (or divide) by a sin/cos/tan value.
If the unknown is the **angle**, take the *inverse* trig function: $\sin^{-1}, \cos^{-1}, \tan^{-1}$ (also written $\arcsin, \arccos, \arctan$).

blocks.title: Common pitfall
blocks.body: When you switch which acute angle you call $\theta$, the **opposite** and **adjacent** labels swap. The hypotenuse stays put. Always re-label *before* picking the ratio.
